title: Arts and Culture
description: Explore Rosehill College's vibrant Arts and Culture programme — visual arts, music, dance, drama, kapa haka and Pasifika groups, with opportunities to perform locally and internationally.
text: Ngā toi Arts and culture Rosehill College offers a vibrant and diverse Arts and Culture programme, providing students with numerous opportunities to excel in various disciplines, from visual arts and music to drama and kapa haka. With a history of national achievements and strong community involvement, students are encouraged to showcase their talents both locally and internationally. Performing arts Dance at Rosehill Dance is offered as an NCEA subject at all levels, featuring opportunities for students to participate in dance shows, school productions, competitions, and cheerleading. Dance at Rosehill College stimulates creativity and fosters self-expression, all while building confidence. In addition to our rich and dynamic curriculum, we offer a range of extra-curricular dance activities, including: NZ Street Dance Megaschool Competition: students compete against other Auckland schools in this prestigious Hip Hop competition held in central Auckland. 'Ascension' Contemporary Dance Group: students develop a repertoire of contemporary dances showcased at the Auckland Cheerleading Competitions. All senior dance classes, performances, and practices take place in our Performing Arts Centre, which features a purpose-built sprung dance floor. Drama at Rosehill Rosehill College's Drama programme boasts a history of academic success and numerous awards. Our highly regarded and inspirational teachers lead annual productions, participate in festivals, theatresports, drama school, and oversee the technical aspects of performances. Drama at Rosehill College fosters critical literacy, enabling students to shape, communicate, and interpret meaning while gaining a deeper understanding of themselves and others. Our diverse extra-curricular activities include: Annual school production/musical: renowned for high-quality plays and musicals, Rosehill College offers students the chance to perform on stage and receive training as backstage crew, lighting and sound teams, props and set makers, and show band members. Held at the Hawkins Theatre in Papakura, these productions provide a semi-professional theatre experience. Our productions have won awards in the Auckland Showdown competition, including Best Props Design (Lion, Witch and the Wardrobe 2011, Fiddler on the Roof 2012) and Best Set Design (The Witches 2013). Theatresports: students master the art of improvisation and compete in the Auckland Theatresports Festival. Rosehill College won the Auckland Shield in 2012 and continues to produce strong teams annually. Sheilah Winn Shakespeare Festival: both student and teacher-directed pieces are entered into this national competition, where students perform scenes before a judging panel. Winners advance to the finals in Wellington and may be selected to visit The Globe Theatre in London. Talent Quest: organised by Rosehill College's Arts Committee, this event showcases the diverse talents of our students at the Hawkins Theatre. Performances include Hip Hop and Contemporary dancers, bands, singers, and stand-up comedians. Performing arts Music at Rosehill At Rosehill College, students can study vocals and band as subjects, and take weekly instrument lessons. Our bands, ensembles, and choirs rehearse regularly to enhance their skills, preparing for competitions and tours both in New Zealand and internationally, with past destinations including Sydney, Argentina, Singapore, and China. Our bands have advanced in Rockquest, performed at Eden Park, and our composers have had their works performed by orchestras. We offer courses at all levels for vocalists and instrumentalists, accommodating both students who can read music and those who cannot. Students enrolled in Music courses can receive free weekly vocal or instrumental lessons from expert itinerant music teachers (subject to availability). All students are encouraged to join co-curricular music groups once they reach the necessary level. Co-curricular music groups Concert Band Includes flutes, ob

title: Alternative Education
description: Rosehill College leads the Alternative Education (Alt Ed) Programme for the Papakura area, providing flexible, supportive learning pathways for students in Years 9–11 in partnership with Blue Light Papakura and Te Awatea.
text: Alt Ed Alternative Education Programme Rosehill College leads the Alternative Education (Alt Ed) Programme for the Papakura area, in partnership with Blue Light Papakura and Te Awatea. About the programme The programme provides flexible, supportive learning pathways for students in Years 9–11 (under 16 years old) who need a tailored environment to succeed. Its purpose is to provide new learning opportunities for students who have become disengaged from mainstream education. This programme is designed for students who have become disengaged from mainstream education and would benefit from a more personalised and supportive learning environment. Enrolments Email: alted@rosehillcollege.school.nz Phone: (09) 295 0661 ext 871 Lead support Mr J Fraser Email: j.fraser@rosehillcollege.school.nz Enrolment process 1. Referral or enquiry Contact the Alt Ed team or speak with your Kaitiaki. 2. Submit enrolment form Complete and return the Alternative Education Enrolment Form. 3. Initial interview Student, whānau, and referring school meet with the Alt Ed team. 4. Transition plan A personalised learning and attendance plan is developed. Programme focus Core learning: literacy, numeracy, and NCEA Foundation Level Pathways: transition planning, employment skills, and work readiness Life skills: hauora, resilience, communication, and teamwork Service and leadership: building social responsibility and whanaungatanga Support: small class sizes, mentoring, and wrap-around pastoral care Programme options (2026) The offsite programmes offer pick-ups and drop-offs, breakfast and lunch, and access to facilities including a fitness centre, music recording, and outdoor education. Alternative Education programme options for 2026 Provider Focus Rosehill College (on site) Re-engagement, foundation learning, transition to school Blue Light Papakura Youth development and leadership Papakura Activity Centre (Te Awatea) Holistic, whānau-based learning and wellbeing support Expected outcomes The programme aims to support students to: Re-engage in education Improve attendance and engagement Develop literacy, numeracy, and communication skills Build confidence, resilience, and self-motivation Transition into further education, training, or employment Downloads Find out more about the programme in our information guide. Alternative Education information guide PDF Apply now To apply, please complete the Alternative Education Enrolment Form and return it to the Alt Ed team at alted@rosehillcollege.school.nz. Email the Alt Ed team Contact us

title: Digital Learning and BYOD
description: Digital learning at Rosehill College — BYOD guidance, recommended Chromebook specifications, our Cybersecurity and BYOD Agreement, and cyber safety resources for whānau.
text: Digital learning Digital learning and BYOD At Rosehill College, we are committed to leveraging digital technologies to enhance our students' learning experiences. As part of this commitment, all students are recommended to have a personal digital device, which they will use to access educational resources, collaborate with peers, and engage in interactive learning activities. Cybersecurity and BYOD Agreement To ensure a safe and effective digital learning environment, we require all parents and caregivers to review and agree to our Cybersecurity and BYOD Agreement before their child is enrolled at Rosehill College. This agreement outlines the responsibilities and expectations for both students and parents regarding the use of digital devices and online behaviour. By adhering to these guidelines, we aim to provide a secure and productive digital learning environment that supports our students' educational growth and prepares them for the future. Please take the time to read through the Cybersecurity and BYOD Agreement to understand the policies and procedures in place to protect and enhance your child's digital learning experience at Rosehill College. Rosehill College digital usage guidelines Cybersecurity and BYOD Agreement Google Drive Phones away for the day Ministry of Education PDF E-learning and choosing a device At Rosehill College, all students benefit from the additional learning opportunities that a digital device provides, making it a necessary learning tool. Each student's device will need to connect to the Rosehill College wireless network, and some devices will be better suited to this task than others. Rosehill College recommends a Chromebook as a functional and affordable tool that gives students access to Google applications, which teachers use to manage resources, lessons, and assessments. Other devices, such as laptops and tablets, will also work within our IT infrastructure. Recommended Chromebook specifications The following minimum specifications for a Chromebook are recommended. Recommended minimum Chromebook specifications Specification Recommended minimum Memory size 2GB Screen size 11.6 inches Screen resolution 1366×768 SSD or HDD capacity 16GB Operating system Chrome OS The Google tools students use Gmail Email service Classroom Software for receiving and submitting classwork Drive Cloud-based office productivity software (documents, spreadsheets, calendar, forms) Meet Online video conferences Sites Website creation software YouTube Video service eLearning at Rosehill College Chromebooks are browser-based devices that provide users with access to the web through the Google Chrome browser, allowing all teachers and students to access the suite of Google tools. The New Zealand Curriculum explicitly states that our vision is to foster young people who will be "confident, connected, actively involved and lifelong learners" (NZC, 2007). The Technology learning area within the New Zealand Curriculum strengthens the positioning of digital technologies to ensure that all learners have the opportunity to become digitally capable individuals. This provides a greater focus on students building their skills so they can be innovative creators of digital solutions, moving beyond solely being users and consumers of digital technologies. Cyber security At Rosehill College, we understand the importance of providing a safe and secure digital environment for our students while also harnessing the vast educational resources available online. title: Junior Diploma
description: The Rosehill College Junior Diploma is our in-house qualification for Year 9, 10 and 11 students, recognising participation, effort and achievement across school life.
text: Years 9–11 Junior Diploma At Rosehill College, we are committed to providing a balanced education that fosters both academic excellence and personal growth. As part of this commitment, we have introduced the Rosehill College Junior Diploma for our Year 9, 10, and 11 students. An in-house qualification with clear goals The Junior Diploma serves as an in-house qualification that fills the gap left by the removal of NCEA Level 1, giving students clear goals to strive for in their early high school years. It is designed to encourage students to embrace our school values, build foundational skills in literacy and numeracy, and prepare for the challenges of senior secondary education. Through the Junior Diploma, students experience a structured framework that rewards their participation, effort, and achievement across all aspects of school life, laying the groundwork for future success. Structure and criteria The Junior Diploma recognises students' achievements across four key areas based on Rosehill College's values: Whanaungatanga (Together we are more) – Encourages school participation through house activities, events, and challenges. Higher engagement leads to higher diploma levels. Manaakitanga (We rise by lifting others) – Promotes respect and cultural involvement. Students participate in haka practice, Manaakitia Mai discussions, and school/community groups. Hihiritanga (We are resilient and persevere) – Focuses on attendance, goal-setting, and academic skills. Students complete our Career Central programme, improve literacy and numeracy, and demonstrate perseverance. Rangatiratanga (Excellence in all that we do) – Recognises academic success. Students accumulate academic points from assessments, with higher diploma levels requiring more points and stronger performance. Achievement levels Achieved Meets all base requirements. Merit Requires higher participation, attendance, and academic performance. Excellence Requires outstanding involvement, leadership, and top academic results. Yearly breakdown Each year level has specific expectations within these categories, ensuring students develop skills progressively. The Junior Diploma is a progressive programme across Year 9, Year 10, and Year 11, with each year building on the previous one. While the core values and categories remain consistent, the specific expectations grow as students mature. Below is an overview of what the Junior Diploma entails at each year level. Year 9 Junior Diploma PDF Year 10 Junior Diploma PDF Year 11 Junior Diploma PDF Future pathways The Junior Diploma prepares students for senior NCEA study and career planning, ensuring they develop essential skills for success. Strong foundation for NCEA – Students enter Year 12 with foundational literacy, numeracy, and study habits, making the transition to NCEA Level 2 smoother. The programme includes NCEA Literacy and Numeracy corequisites, reducing future workload. Career planning and goal-setting – Through our Career Central programme, students explore career options, set learning goals, and plan their Year 12 subjects accordingly. Leadership and responsibility – Encourages participation in clubs, sports, and leadership roles, helping students develop teamwork, time management, and initiative — valuable traits for tertiary study and employment. Early NCEA credits – Year 11 students earn Level 2 Unit Standards (11 credits), providing a head start on NCEA Level 2. Guidance and support – Teachers and our career advisor help students refine their study plans, explore scholarships, and prepare for their future education or vocational pathways. Ultimately, the Rosehill College Junior Diploma prepares students for life beyond junior high school. It ensures they are academically competent and have the qualifications needed to progress, it instils in them the importance of participation and community, and it sets them on a path of thoughtful career planning. title: Learning Areas and Subjects
description: Explore the learning areas and subjects at Rosehill College, from English, the Arts and Humanities to Languages, Mathematics, Science, Te Reo Māori, Physical Education and Health, and Technology.
text: Ngā wāhanga ako Learning areas and subjects Our curriculum spans English, the Arts, Humanities, Languages, Mathematics, Physical Education and Health, Science, Te Reo Māori, Technology and ESOL. The Arts Visual arts Year 9: In both the Year 9 courses — Art and Design, students will have the opportunity to explore some art-making conventions, learn about and apply knowledge of elements of art and develop competence within the essential skills through the use of materials and processes. Drawing is the primary means for students to develop and create art works, developing and revisiting their ideas using various approaches in response to a variety of motivations, observations, and imagination, supported by the study of artists' work. In Art there is an emphasis on working within the fields of painting and printmaking, and in Design an emphasis on the fields of sculpture and design, although students will explore aspects of all four fields in both courses. Year 10: In Art and Design, students continue to develop and extend their visual, literate and technical skills within a variety of themes. Drawing is used to underpin the exploration of a variety of two and three dimensional media, encouraging students to pursue and challenge their own creative processes through learning about art, communicating visual ideas and making original work. The courses provide a solid foundation of skill-building in preparation for entry into Level 1 Art History, Visual Art and Photography and Design the following year. Year 11: Year 11 students can study either Visual Arts or Art Design. These courses build on and extend the skills taught in Year 10 Art and Year 10 Art Design. Year 12: Year 12 courses are offered in Art Design, Painting, Printmaking and Drawing, and Photography. These courses build on the skills taught in Year 11 Photography and Design and Visual Arts. Students may take up to two of these practical courses on successful completion of either Year 11 Art course. The Arts Dance Dance students integrate thinking, moving and feeling. They explore and use dance elements, vocabularies, processes and technologies to express personal, group and cultural identities. Students develop literacy in Dance as they learn about and develop skills in performing, choreographing and responding to a variety of genres from a range of historical and contemporary contexts. The Arts Music At Rosehill College we offer Music courses to cater for all! Free weekly lessons are available for Music students, but there may be a waiting list. Check out the instruments that you can learn here at Rosehill College: flute, oboe, clarinet, bass clarinet, saxophone, cornet, trumpet, horn, trombone, tuba, piano, vocals, guitar, bass guitar, and drumkit. Enjoy your musical adventure! title: Mobile Phone Policy
description: Rosehill College's mobile phone policy — phones away for the day, guidelines for students, consequences for misuse, and advice for parents and caregivers.
text: Phones away for the day Mobile phone policy At Rosehill College, we believe mobile phones are not necessary during the school day. However, we understand that some parents and caregivers want their children to have a phone for after-school communication. Why we have this policy Under the New Zealand Legislation of Education (School Boards) Regulation 2020, the Rosehill College Board has the duty to prohibit the use or access of mobile phones at school. If a student brings a phone to school, the following guidelines apply. This policy helps keep our school a safe, respectful, and focused learning environment. Rosehill College digital usage guidelines Cybersecurity and BYOD Agreement Phones away for the day — whānau FAQs (Ministry of Education) The policy in detail General guidelines The school is not responsible for lost, damaged, or stolen phones. Phones must be on silent and kept in school bags between 8.45am and 3.10pm. Teachers may allow supervised use of phones for learning activities. Phones may be brought on EOTC activities, at the discretion of staff and in line with school policies. In an emergency, parents should contact the school office, and we will pass the message on to students. Parents/caregivers can call or email the Student Office if they need to contact their child during the day. Consequences for misuse If a phone is seen during class or break times, it may be confiscated by a Head of House or any SLT member. It will be kept at the Student Office and collected by a parent, caregiver, or student at the end of the school week (Friday). Phones may be confiscated if they disrupt learning or break school guidelines. Collection is available Fridays between 3.15–4.15pm from the Student Office. Early collection can only be arranged through the Principal by emailing the Principal's Secretary at pa@rosehillcollege.school.nz . Please arrange this in advance. Continued violations may result in a parent/student meeting with a Kaitiaki or Deputy Principal and could be treated as persistent defiance. Inappropriate use during school hours Examples of inappropriate mobile phone use include: Bullying or disrespectful messages about staff or students Taking photos/videos of others without permission Sharing inappropriate content Disrupting learning in class or for others The school will investigate incidents through our discipline procedure. Parents will be informed, and appropriate consequences will be applied depending on the behaviour. Guidelines for parents — use outside of school Parents and caregivers are responsible for their children's phone use outside school hours. We encourage families to: Set clear rules about phone use at home Encourage children to be the same person online and in person Monitor passwords, limit Wi-Fi use overnight, and stay engaged with your child's online activity If online incidents occur If online incidents occur involving Rosehill College students: Reach out to the other student's parents in a calm way to resolve the matter together If behaviour breaches the Harmful Digital Communications Act 2015, please contact Netsafe or the New Zealand Police For further support, resources are available from: Netsafe — www.netsafe.org.nz The Parenting Place — www.parentingplace.nz Need to reach your child during the day? Parents and caregivers can call or email the Student Office, and in an emergency the school office will pass a message on to your child. Contact us Digital learning and BYOD

title: Our Rosehill College Way
description: Discover the Rosehill College Way, including our values of Manaakitanga, Rangatiratanga, Whanaungatanga, and Hihiritanga. Learn about our expectations for uniform and attendance, including our attendance management plan, and how we foster a positive, inclusive environment that supports student success and personal growth.
text: Values and expectations Our Rosehill College Way Manaakitanga, Rangatiratanga, Whanaungatanga and Hihiritanga — four values that shape how we connect, reflect, respect and strive every day, alongside clear expectations for attendance and uniform. What do we expect from you? We expect students to attend school every day, as consistent attendance is crucial for developing lifelong learning skills and achieving higher educational outcomes, which contribute to better health, higher incomes, job stability, and stronger, more connected communities. At Rosehill College, we also expect students to be in their uniform as it fosters a sense of belonging and pride within our school community. Wearing a uniform promotes equality, reduces distractions, and helps create a focused learning environment. It reflects our commitment to maintaining high standards and discipline, ensuring that all students present themselves in a respectful and unified manner. Uniforms also prepare students for future professional settings where dress codes are common, instilling a sense of responsibility and readiness for the world beyond school. Uniform expectation Seniors uniform The expectation for students to wear their uniforms at Rosehill College is grounded in the belief that it promotes unity and school spirit. Uniforms eliminate socio-economic disparities, allowing students to focus on their education rather than fashion. Juniors uniform Wearing our uniform supports a disciplined atmosphere and enhances the school's identity, making students easily recognisable as members of our institution. It also encourages students to take pride in their appearance and fosters a professional mindset that benefits them in future academic and career endeavours. Uniform and stationery Every day counts One or two days a week does not seem that much, but the missed learning adds up — and children learn best at the start of the day, so arriving late to school or class matters too. title: Student Support
description: Student support at Rosehill College — Learner Support, Gifted Education, Careers guidance and the Gateway work experience programme.
text: Here to help Student support We are dedicated to fostering a nurturing and inclusive environment for all our students. Our comprehensive support system is designed to address the diverse needs of our students, ensuring they have access to the resources and assistance necessary to thrive both academically and personally. At Rosehill College, we understand that every student is unique, and our dedicated team is here to help. Whether you need academic guidance, mental health support, or assistance with personal challenges, we are committed to providing a safe and supportive space for you to share your concerns. Rosehill College is a KidsLink school. Kidslink connects schools and families with professional services for children and teens. Learner Support The role of Learner Support is to: Assist with transition of students with SEN from year 8 to year 9. Provide support for students with identified Special Educational Needs (SEN) in years 9 to 13. Carry out evaluations of students with suspected SEN. Liaise with parents and caregivers. Provide advice and guidance to teachers on SEN matters. Liaise with support agencies providing services to students with SEN, their caregivers and their teachers. If you have any queries please contact the Head of Learning Support on extension 873. Gifted Education Rosehill College recognises that our student population is diverse. An enriched learning environment is the right of all students and is essential for the development of their gifts and talents. We offer a range of approaches, including advanced courses, acceleration of content delivery, involvement in subject competitions (e.g., Mathex, NSW Science, and the Future Problem Solving National competition), and visits from speakers focusing on goal setting, motivation, philosophy, effective learning strategies, and achieving excellence. The school's high-ability students at the senior level are encouraged to pursue academic excellence and sit for NZQA Scholarship examinations. Depending on the subject area, enrolment in university papers for extension purposes is also available. The College defines giftedness as encompassing various areas of learning, including intellectual, physical, creative, spiritual, and leadership domains. The identification of our gifted students is inclusive and ongoing, using a multi-method approach. These methods include, but are not limited to, teacher observations using characteristic checklists or observation scales, questionnaires, standardised tests (e.g. asTTle), parent referrals, peer referrals, and self-referrals. The most common identification approach is teacher observation and nomination. An elite sports programme is also offered by the College, along with numerous opportunities for involvement in the visual arts, drama, music, and Kapa Haka performances. Gifted Education at Rosehill College Careers The aim of Careers Education at Rosehill College is to provide appropriate career education, information, and guidance for students. The Careers Centre is available for student use before school, during intervals, at lunchtime, and after school, as well as during study time for Year 13 students. It contains information about a wide range of careers and courses and is staffed by Ms Ferguson, the Careers Adviser, and Ms Mowday, the Careers Assistant. Computers with internet access are also available. Students can visit the Careers Room to receive expert advice and assistance to aid in their choices regarding tertiary education and employment opportunities. Assistance is also available for writing CVs, seeking work experience, applying for scholarships or Halls of Residence, and completing applications to tertiary institutions. Additionally, the Careers Department hosts a variety of lunchtime seminars to provide information about tertiary institutions, student loans, Armed Forces, etc. These seminars are advertised in the daily bulletin and on the Careers Bulletin on the school website. title: Year 9 SMART Assessment
description: Information for Year 9 whānau about the SMART assessment at Rosehill College — what it is, why it matters, and how students can prepare, including the assessment dates of 4–15 May.
text: Year 9 Year 9 SMART assessment — May 4–15 The SMART (Student Monitoring, Assessment and Reporting Tool) is a new online assessment. It is being introduced across schools in Aotearoa New Zealand from 2026, replacing the e-asTTle assessment. Key details at a glance Dates Monday 4 – Friday 15 May Where Students complete the assessment in the Hall Duration Each assessment typically takes 30–45 minutes Devices Assessments are completed online — students should bring their own device. Parents can let Kaitiaki/Head of House know if a device needs to be booked. Please do so by 4th May. SMART familiarisation site (recommended first step) Students are encouraged to explore the SMART platform before completing the assessment. SMART Familiarisation Site This site allows students to: Try sample questions Become familiar with the assessment format Build confidence before the actual assessment What is the SMART assessment? The SMART (Student Monitoring, Assessment and Reporting Tool) is a new online assessment. It is being introduced across schools in Aotearoa New Zealand from 2026. SMART provides clear and consistent information about student progress in: Reading Writing Mathematics It replaces the e-asTTle assessment and is aligned with the new curriculum. Teachers use SMART results to track student progress and support conversations with students and whānau. Parents and caregivers will receive information about their child's progress through our reporting processes. Purpose of the assessment SMART is a learning check designed to: Help teachers understand what students already know Identify next learning steps Provide information to parents and caregivers The focus is on progress and improvement over time, rather than a single result. Further testing is occurring in Term 4. How the assessment runs Assessments are completed online, students should bring their own device. Each assessment typically takes 30–45 minutes. Questions may include: Multiple choice Short written responses Interactive tasks (for example, drag-and-drop tools) Students complete the assessment in the Hall. Parents can let Kaitiaki/Head of House know if a device needs to be booked. Please do so by 4th May. Further information For more information about SMART, please refer to the official guidance from the Ministry of Education. If you have any questions, please contact the college. title: Applying and New Zealand Arrival Information
description: Get all the information you need for applying as an international student at Rosehill College and preparing for your arrival in New Zealand. Learn about the application process, visa requirements, and the steps to take before you arrive.
text: Nau mai, haere mai Applying and arrival information Auckland awaits — your ultimate study destination. Named the 10th best city in the world by the 2023 Global Liveability Index and located in the 4th safest country according to the Global Peace Index, Auckland is a place where you can study, live, and thrive. Your study destination Papakura/Karaka is a charming and safe suburb, perfect for those seeking a quieter lifestyle. This area, which is rapidly growing, features high-quality housing and is close to beautiful beaches, large parks, and great shopping malls. Downtown Auckland, offering incredible dining, shopping, and entertainment options, is just a short 30-minute drive or ferry ride away. Auckland enjoys a pleasant climate with four distinct seasons. Summers see temperatures in the mid-20s°C, while winters are cool, with temperatures in the mid-10s°C. Application process Rosehill College welcomes international students who wish to gain internationally accepted qualifications, in an environment designed specifically to assist and guide them to achieve to their fullest potential. We are now accepting applications for this year and next. We know choosing a school is a big decision for both parents and students. Apply to study — Want to study in New Zealand and experience the Kiwi lifestyle? Start your international student application with Rosehill College today . What you will need — To complete the online process, you will need to upload the following documents: A copy of your passport (and visa if you have one) Your photo Your last school report Insurance certificate and policy details in English (if you are booking your own) Application — Once your application is submitted it will be reviewed within 48 hours. We will contact you or your agent directly with the next steps. Acceptance and visa — Once accepted, we will send you a letter with an Offer of Place and an invoice, which can be used to start your visa application process, if required. Once payment is received, we will issue a receipt and a Confirmed Offer of Place to help you finalise your visa application. You will need to book a return flight to secure a visa. Apply for your visa online How to apply for a New Zealand student visa For a visual guide on how to apply, this tutorial walks you through each step of the process. By following these steps, you can successfully apply for your New Zealand student visa and get ready to begin your journey at Rosehill College. Arrival information Welcome to Rosehill College! Thank you for choosing our school. We are excited to welcome you to our community and look forward to meeting you soon. To help you prepare for your journey and ensure a smooth transition, here is some important information about what to bring, what to expect upon arrival in New Zealand, and your first days at school. What to bring Passport and visa: Ensure your passport is valid and that you have a current visa to study in New Zealand. Insurance: Carry your travel and medical insurance policy documents with you. Return ticket: Have your return airline ticket ready. Medications: Bring any necessary medications and their prescriptions. Electronics: Don't forget your laptop, mobile phone, and proof of purchase/receipts for these items. Bring an adapter plug suitable for New Zealand's electrical outlets. Personal items and clothes: Pack clothes appropriate for New Zealand's variable climate, and any personal items you might need. Spending money: Bring some local currency, but avoid carrying large amounts of cash. Arrival in New Zealand Flight details: Please inform us of your flight and arrival details so that we can arrange for someone to meet you at Auckland Airport. Pack your own bags: Do not carry items for other people, and ensure you pack your bags yourself. Arrival process: Upon arrival, you will need to complete a Passenger Arrival Card. Declare all food items, even if they are packaged, as fresh food is not allowed into New Zealand. Customs and i

title: Curriculum for International Students
description: Explore the curriculum offerings for international students at Rosehill College — over 40 subjects, NCEA Levels 2 and 3, ESOL support and Chinese interpreters.
text: Te marautanga Curriculum for international students The school curriculum is core to student life in New Zealand at Rosehill College. International students are integrated into regular classes with local students and can choose from over 40 subject options in Year 12 and 13, including our ever-popular Outdoor Education course. A pathway to tertiary study ESOL support is provided where needed and we have Chinese interpreters available. Our Year 12 and 13 courses give students the opportunity to gain New Zealand's NCEA qualification at Levels 2 and 3, providing a pathway to tertiary study. NCEA is an internationally recognised qualification accepted by universities worldwide. Year 11 students study a curriculum tailored to provide a strong foundation for the rigours of NCEA in Year 12. All subjects at this level offer NCEA-style evaluations, to ensure students are ready for external assessments in Year 12. View course information Ō mātau hua Our results The success of our international students is very important to us. Our International and Careers Departments work closely with students to ensure they are enrolled in appropriate courses, taking the correct number of credits, and are on track to achieve. 93% of international students who sat NCEA Levels 2 and 3 gained their qualification (2023) 100% of students who sat University Entrance obtained it (2023) 40+ subject options in Year 12 and 13 Ngā panonitanga What happened to NCEA Level 1? Starting in 2024, significant changes are being implemented in the NCEA (National Certificate of Educational Achievement) system in New Zealand, particularly for NCEA Level 1. These changes are part of a broader initiative to improve the quality, fairness, and clarity of the qualification system. Key changes to NCEA Level 1: Credit requirements: NCEA Level 1 shifts from requiring 80 credits to 60 credits. This change is designed to reduce the assessment load on students and emphasise deeper learning. Literacy and numeracy co-requisite: A new 20-credit literacy and numeracy co-requisite has been introduced. In addition to the 60 credits required for Level 1, students must also achieve 20 credits specifically in literacy and numeracy. These credits are separate from the main 60-credit requirement and must be completed for students to be awarded their NCEA qualification. Phasing out old standards: The current Level 1 achievement standards expired at the end of 2023, with the new standards fully implemented in 2024. No credit carry-over: The ability to carry over 20 credits from one level to the next has been removed. Each NCEA level now requires students to achieve the full 60 credits independently. These changes are part of the ongoing effort by the New Zealand Ministry of Education to enhance the quality of education and ensure that students are better prepared for further education, training, or entering the workforce. At Rosehill College, our Junior Diploma gives Year 9, 10 and 11 students clear goals to strive for as they build towards NCEA. Ready to study with us? This page is designed to help you understand the costs associated with your education and the policies that govern your experience as a member of our college community. Planning your budget Here, you will find detailed information about tuition fees, accommodation costs, and other related expenses, along with essential policies regarding payment schedules, refunds, and academic requirements. Our goal is to ensure that you are fully informed and prepared to make the most of your educational journey at Rosehill College. Whether you are planning your budget, reviewing payment options, or seeking clarity on specific policies, this page will guide you through the financial and procedural aspects of studying with us. title: Living in Aotearoa
description: Discover what it's like to live in New Zealand as an international student. From the vibrant culture and stunning landscapes to practical information on accommodation, transport, and daily life, this page offers everything you need to know to make the most of your time in New Zealand while studying at Rosehill College.
text: International students Living in Aotearoa Welcome to New Zealand, a country renowned for its stunning landscapes, vibrant cities, and welcoming communities. As an international student at Rosehill College, you will have the opportunity to experience all that this beautiful country has to offer, both inside and outside the classroom. Real students, real stories Life as an international student at Rosehill College Experience what life is really like at Rosehill College and in Auckland — real students, real stories, and the adventures that make studying in New Zealand unforgettable. Accommodation If you are staying with a homestay family, they will collect you from the airport when you arrive, show you around your new home, and help you settle in. You should arrive a couple of days before your course starts so you can acclimatise. For more information visit our page on arrival information . Most of our international students live with a local host family while they are in New Zealand. Our homestay provides warm and caring host families who offer safe, comfortable and well-maintained homes, providing students with a secure and supportive living environment. The families are carefully selected by our dedicated and experienced staff. Homestay accommodation gives our international students the opportunity to fully participate in New Zealand life and make the most of their Rosehill College experience. Rosehill College homestays All international students not living with their parents must live in a school-approved homestay. Students are not permitted to live in flats/apartments. Regardless of age, they must live with a family where approved adults care for them. Some parents may elect to place their child with relatives or close family friends. Such homes (designated caregivers) are subject to the usual checks carried out for approved homestays. All accommodation will be regularly visited by Rosehill College homestay staff. All Rosehill College homestay carers and designated caregivers and their live-in family members over the age of 18 have been vetted by the New Zealand Police. Fees are paid to the school for the same period as the tuition fees. The school pays the homestay families on a two-weekly basis. The homestay weekly rate includes an amount that is used by the College to monitor the homestay. Students may not change their accommodation without the Director's permission, and this permission must be sought in writing and well in advance. Your homestay family Your family has been specially chosen for you. They will act in place of your parents while you are in NZ. Make sure you have completed our questionnaire so that we understand your needs. All families have different habits and customs. It is important to find out what your new family expects of you. You will be given a booklet called Homestay Information and Guidance for International Students. You should read and discuss this booklet with your family. It includes a list of useful questions you might ask, such as what to call them, when to have a shower and what happens with your washing. You will have your own room. You should ask them about use of the computer/internet. You will need to get a NZ SIM card for your phone and will need to open a NZ bank account. Your homestay family or our International Co-ordinator will help you with these. Your family will show you how to get to school. NB: You are not allowed to own nor drive a car while you are an international student at Rosehill College. Most parents work (mother and father). Students choosing not to stay in Rosehill College homestays These students must live with either their parents or a close family relative or friend. These students' parents must sign the 'Statement of Designated Caregivers Arrangements' form, available from the school or agents, stating that they agree to take responsibility for placing their son/daughter with their relative. Students living with their parents do not have to complete the Designat

title: Why Study at Rosehill?
description: Discover why studying at Rosehill College is a great choice for international students. Our welcoming environment, diverse curriculum, and strong support services make it the ideal place to grow academically, socially, and personally.
text: International students Why study at Rosehill? Rosehill College, Auckland, New Zealand has shaped and influenced the lives of young men and women since 1970 — a proud co-educational college committed to providing a high-quality educational environment that will ensure every student is successful and strives to gain academic excellence. The Rosehill experience Rosehill College is based on traditional values of Respect, Responsibility and Caring with innovative approaches to teaching and learning, supported by modern information communication technology. It is our intention to produce graduates that are confident, self-reliant, and have self-respect and respect for others. International students come from all over the world and diversity is a strong focus at Rosehill College. Both domestic and international students enjoy learning about different cultures and believe that this multicultural focus prepares them well for life in the global community. We celebrate our coeducational and bicultural identity and the opportunities presented to establish healthy friendships with people from a diverse range of backgrounds which is Auckland today. Warm and welcoming atmosphere Known for its friendly community, Rosehill College provides a welcoming environment that eases the transition for international students, helping them feel at home away from home. Our reputable education system aligns with international standards, ensuring a seamless transition for international students and preparing them for global academic success. Cultural familiarity With a growing local international student community, Rosehill College offers a sense of cultural familiarity through events, celebrations, and activities that allow international students to stay connected to their roots. Tailored English language support programmes assist international students in improving their language skills, boosting their confidence in communication and academic performance. Safe and secure environment Rosehill College's location in a safe neighbourhood provides peace of mind to international students and their families, ensuring a secure setting for focused learning with excellent homestay families. We carefully select host families who are known for their warmth and hospitality, ensuring a nurturing environment for our international students. Counselling and guidance Dedicated counselling services offer guidance on academic, personal, and cultural matters, ensuring international students receive the support they need to excel and thrive. Rosehill College's strong connections with the New Zealand universities and local international communities provide networking opportunities for current students, facilitating valuable connections for future career prospects. title: Enrolment
description: Learn about enrolling at Rosehill College. Find all the information you need on application requirements, key dates, and the step-by-step process to join our vibrant school community. Start your journey with us today and become part of the Rosehill College family.
text: Join us Rosehill College enrolment We are excited to help you join our thriving learning community. In-zone students apply online; out-of-zone applicants collect an enrolment pack from reception and enter the annual ballot. Enrolments for the following school year typically open at the start of Term 3 each year. For all enrolment enquiries, please email enrolments@rosehillcollege.school.nz. Start new enrolment application Email the enrolment team In-zone enrolment In-zone enrolment All students who live within the Rosehill College zone are entitled to enrol at the College. Complete your application online for a fast and straightforward process — you can check if you are in zone for Rosehill College by entering your address. If you do not have access to an electronic device, computers are available at our front office for you to complete your online enrolment. Our front office staff are also available to assist you with the process. Start new enrolment application In-zone enrolment, step by step Before you start We strongly encourage you to complete the enrolment application online, ideally on a desktop computer or laptop for the best experience. Please note: submitting your application online does not guarantee automatic acceptance. All submissions will be reviewed, and you will receive confirmation via email once the process is complete. To get started, download the required forms (which can be filled out electronically using a Microsoft Edge browser or Adobe Acrobat for Drive). Be sure to save the completed forms, as they will be uploaded to the 'Attachments' section later in this application. If you are unable to upload and submit the required forms and supporting documents online, you can complete the application with just the information and details. You may then bring the documents to the school office or email them separately to enrolments@rosehillcollege.school.nz . Our front office staff are available to assist with scanning and entering your documents into our system. If you prefer to complete the enrolment process using paper forms, you can collect an enrolment pack from our front office. The office is open from 8am to 4pm, Monday to Friday . Check your school zone All students who live within the Rosehill College zone are entitled to enrol at the College. You can check if you are in zone for Rosehill College by entering your address at the following link: Check for your school zone Enrolment forms The following forms are compulsory and must be submitted. Please download and fill out each form, ensuring they are completed in full. Note: All forms require a parent or guardian's signature. It is essential that all required signatures are included — any unsigned forms will not be processed. The compulsory forms are as follows: Student Health Record In-zone Enrolment Questionnaire Education Outside the Classroom (EOTC) Consent Form Download the checklist Download the forms Please ensure all forms are signed and submitted to avoid any delays in the enrolment process. We also ask that you take a moment to read through Our Rosehill College Way , which outlines our school-wide rules and expectations. It is important that both you and your child/children familiarise yourselves with these guidelines before completing the enrolment. This helps ensure a smooth transition and supports a positive, respectful learning environment for all. Supporting documents To facilitate the processing of enrolment applications, we kindly request digital copies of the following documents, preferably in PDF format. Compulsory documents: Birth Certificate or Passport Student's Visa: for students who are not New Zealand/Australian citizens. Legal or Guardianship documents: any legal documents (e.g. Guardianship, Parenting Order, Custody Order, Protection Order), if applicable. Proof of Address: TWO different documents, each with the caregiver's name, supply address or rating unit where the student lives, and with the most recent date. Tenancy Agree

title: Community Outreach
description: Rosehill College students make a positive difference through community outreach. Join our Student Council, Interact Club, or Community Committee.
text: Whanaungatanga Community outreach We get involved to make a difference! At Rosehill College, we are committed to making a positive difference in our community. Through partnerships, service projects, and outreach programmes, our students develop leadership skills, empathy, and a strong sense of responsibility while creating lasting connections with the wider community. There is a place for you These amazing student groups at Rosehill College are making a lasting impact through their hard work, dedication, and passion. Whether you are interested in supporting causes, leading events, or working as a team, there is a place for you! Join, support, and find out more about how you can get involved. The Senior Student Community Committee The Senior Community Committee at Rosehill College is dedicated to driving positive change by supporting both local and global causes. Through initiatives like Pink Shirt Day, the CanTeen Colour Run, and the World Vision 40-Hour Challenge, this group works tirelessly to make a meaningful impact. Our senior students are committed to fighting bullying, raising funds for cancer research, and supporting efforts to address climate change. By joining this group, you can be part of something that truly makes a difference. Interact Club The Interact Club is a mixed year level group for all students to join. They are dedicated to fundraising for charities and supporting global and local causes. Throughout the year, they organise exciting events like Valentine's Day fundraisers, Easter chocolate sales, and the Mother's Day bake sale, raising money for important causes such as Women's Refuge, Foster Hope, and Breast Cancer Research. The club strengthens community connections by engaging with the Rotary Club. If you enjoy fundraising, teamwork, and giving back to the community, the Interact Club is the perfect place for you! Join up by sending a DM to the group's Instagram page, Rosehill College Interact Club, to support impactful charities and make a difference together! Student Council The Student Council is a mixed year level group that plays an important role in bringing the Rosehill College community together. In 2024, they hosted the first-ever Rosehill College Community Gala, a successful event that united students, families, and the Papakura community. From organising events to representing your voice in school decisions, the Student Council has a major role in shaping school culture and organising key events within our school. If you are passionate about leadership and making your school experience unforgettable, consider joining the Student Council. Support the community, get involved, and help make Rosehill College an even better place — please contact Mrs Powell at m.powell@rosehillcollege.school.nz to learn more. Together, we can continue to make Rosehill College a place of positive change and community spirit! Rosehill College yearly Christmas Hamper Project Since 2024, the Community Committee has made an incredible impact through the Christmas Hamper Project, expanding its efforts to support not only Rosehill College families but also those from neighbouring schools. Following the heartwarming success of the initiative, the team, led by Business Manager Ms Carolissen, has worked tirelessly to broaden its reach. Thanks to the generous sponsorship of Life Church and the kindness of our staff, students, and families, we have already raised a significant amount of funding. This has enabled us to provide hampers for families facing hardship during the Christmas period. We are truly overwhelmed by the community's outpouring of support. It is both inspiring and deeply appreciated, and we are grateful for every donation that makes this project possible. If you would like to be part of this wonderful effort and help brighten a family's Christmas, please consider making a donation through the year. Every contribution helps to bring joy to those in need. Thank you for your generosity and for helping us sprea

title: Our Student Leaders
description: Discover the inspiring student leaders at Rosehill College. Our student leaders play a vital role in shaping a positive school culture, encouraging school spirit, and representing the student body. Meet the dedicated young leaders who are committed to making a difference in our school community.
text: Ngā kaiārahi ākonga Our student leaders 2026 At Rosehill College, our student leaders embody the values and spirit of our school community. Committed to excellence, integrity, and service, these exceptional students take on leadership roles to inspire and guide their peers. Each leader brings unique strengths and perspectives, contributing to the vibrant and inclusive atmosphere of our school. On this page, you can learn more about our student leaders, their responsibilities, and their personal quotes that reflect their individual philosophies and aspirations. These quotes serve as a source of inspiration and motivation for all members of our school community. Discover the voices of our student leaders and see how they are making a positive impact at Rosehill College. Head Student Arnav Chandra Rosehill College is more than just a place of learning. It is a kura where potential is recognised, character is shaped, and every student is encouraged to become the best version of themselves. Here, we are given opportunities not only to succeed academically, but to grow as people. We are challenged to step forward, take responsibility, and lead with integrity. At Rosehill, leadership is not about titles. It is about action, respect, and the choices we make every day. As Head Student, I see a school filled with talent, resilience, and ambition. Our strength lies in our diversity, our willingness to support one another, and our courage to aim high even when the path is challenging. With the guidance of our teachers and the support of our peers, we are never alone in our journey. A quote that inspires me is this: “Do not wait for opportunity. Create it.” Together, let us create a culture of excellence, kindness, and courage. If we believe in ourselves and each other, there is nothing we cannot achieve. Head Student Addison Hall Rosehill College has taught me that growth doesn’t just happen by accident, it happens when you choose to step forward. Our kura has taught me far more than how to achieve; it’s taught me how to keep going. I’ve learned that progress matters more than perfection, because no one begins perfect. Leadership to me, begins with putting in that effort, slowly making that progress, to inspire others to take their first step. In particular, our school value of Hihiritanga has stuck with me throughout my time here. The core belief that putting in the effort even when it’s hard to, maintaining that resilience to achieve our goals is something I hold with me going into 2026. Through the vast opportunities, challenges and people around me, I have grown not only as a student and a leader, but as a person. As Head Student, I want to give back to the school that has shaped who I am. I believe leadership is about building momentum, inspiring others to step up, take chances and work towards the goals that matter to them. Rosehill College doesn’t just prepare us for the future, it shapes who we become along the way. “Ko te pae tawhiti whāia kia tata, ko te pae tata whakamaua kia tīna.” Head Student Arpit Behal Here, at Rosehill College, we prioritise culture and our ‘way’ of doing things; framed by the four core values that consist of Manaakitanga, Hihiritanga, Rangatiratanga and Whanaungatanga. Alongside this rich culture, at our kura, ākonga are exposed to a diverse range of subjects, clubs and experiences. Within these experiences, students are given encouragement to tackle each and every hurdle with Hihiritanga. It is instilled in them to always strive for excellence (Rangatiratanga) and to treat every individual with respect; constantly exemplifying Whanaungatanga. All, while demonstrating Manaakitanga through the connections they make. Stepping into my fifth year of high school, I realise the growth that I've had as an individual. The experiences I've had here, the people I have met and the opportunities I have taken, have shaped my values, beliefs and my work ethic. It is this growth that makes me proud to represent Rosehil

title: Student Welfare
description: Explore the Student Welfare services at Rosehill College, dedicated to supporting the well-being, safety, and development of our students. Our welfare team offers guidance, resources, and a caring environment to help students thrive academically, socially, and emotionally throughout their school journey.
text: Hauora Student welfare At Rosehill College, the well-being of our students is our top priority. This page provides essential information and resources to support our students in various aspects of their school life. Our canteen Our canteen is currently operated by the school to ensure students continue to have access to fresh and affordable food options throughout the day. Our menu includes a variety of hot meals, sandwiches, snacks, and drinks, with options rotating regularly and seasonally to suit student preferences. We aim to provide nutritious and convenient food choices to support student wellbeing and energy levels during the school day. If you have any inquiries for the canteen, you can email canteen@rosehillcollege.school.nz . Menu and allergen information The current canteen menu and price list, along with the list of allergens, can be downloaded below. Canteen menu and price list PDF Allergens list PDF Restorative practice Restorative practices are best understood as a 'community response' to wrong-doing. This involves bringing together all of the people involved in an incident to understand how people have been affected, with a focus on putting it right. Why is Rosehill College using this approach? Educators are recognising that restorative practices have many advantages over punishment alone: Restorative practices have a focus on people, not rules. Restorative practices give victims a voice about how they want their case to be handled. Restorative practices make students who offend accountable to the people they have hurt. Restorative practices address the root cause of an issue, not just the symptoms. How do restorative practices fit with our values? Perfectly! Rosehill College is a community of students, parents and staff — so having a community or hui response to wrong-doing is ideal. Restorative practices place a priority on protecting relationships and rebuilding them if they become damaged through a harmful incident. Restorative practices are built upon core values such as Respect, Responsibility and Caring — which match our own values. Student Support Student Support is the department at Rosehill College dedicated to the welfare of students. It is staffed by three full-time counsellors, two full-time nurses and a Kaimanaaki (Māori support). Our three experienced counsellors (Dr Elahe Khaleghian, Ms Phillipa MacCormick and Miss Paula Alontave) are full members of the New Zealand Association of Counsellors, receiving regular supervision of their work. They have contact with a wide range of agencies who can also assist with more complicated issues for students. Our registered nurse, Mrs Serena Li, attends to emergencies, general illness and chronic illnesses. Parents are advised to keep their children at home if they are significantly unwell. Our Kaimanaaki (Whāea Pare Matthews) provides general support to Māori students and their whānau. The role of our Kaimanaaki is to provide support that allows Māori students to succeed to their potential. Visiting Student Support Making appointments Students can 'drop in' during break times and may be able to find the person they are wanting to talk to. It is probably best for students to fill in an appointment slip with their details — the counsellor, Kaimanaaki or nurse will send for them using a runner. Programmes and groups There are always speciality support groups running for students. Students should drop in to Student Support to check, or just keep an eye on the Daily Notices. Peer Support Programme Dr Khaleghian runs this programme with the support of teaching staff and 100–200 Student Advisors. Student Advisors are trustworthy and friendly seniors from each House who train for two days during the summer holidays. Once trained, the Student Advisors support Year 9s on their first day at school, for all of Term One and beyond. title: About Us
description: Explore Rosehill College's core values and educational vision.
text: Ko wai mātau Our vision and values Rosehill College, officially opened in 1970, has a proud history of outstanding academic achievement and a record of national success in both sports and the performing arts. Mission and vision Our Mission Statement is: "Together we create an environment for personal excellence." Our Vision Statement is: "Rosehill College will nurture a culture of excellence through Hihiritanga, Rangatiratanga, Whanaungatanga, and Manaakitanga." Hihiritanga: Strive — we are resilient and persevere Rangatiratanga: Reflect — excellence in all that we do Whanaungatanga: Respect — together we are more Manaakitanga: Connect — we rise by lifting others 1970 Officially opened Year 9–13 Co-educational college 13–18 Ages we serve Papakura Ngāti Tamaoho rohe Mō mātau About us Rosehill College is a co-educational secondary school with a rich history of providing quality education and fostering a supportive learning environment. The College sits within the Ngāti Tamaoho rohe. This area extends from north of the Tamaki Isthmus to south of the Waikato River, to the Whangamarino wetlands. It extends from the West Coast to the Hauraki Gulf/Firth of Thames. Its interests are concentrated in the Manukau lowlands, Āwhitu Peninsula, Papakura, Hunua Ranges, Mangatangi, and Whangamarino. Ngāti Tamaoho has a rich cultural heritage, including traditions, stories, and practices passed down through generations. They have a significant connection to their land and natural resources, and like other iwi, they play an active role in the social, cultural, and economic life of their communities. Rosehill College serves students from Year 9 to Year 13 (ages 13 to 18). The college offers a wide range of academic, sporting, cultural, and extracurricular activities to cater to the diverse interests and needs of its students. Established in 1970, Rosehill College has a rich history of providing quality education and fostering a supportive learning environment. The school emphasises holistic development, aiming to nurture students not only academically but also socially, emotionally, and physically. Rapua ētahi atu Keep exploring Our Rosehill College Way How our four values shape every day at school. Our history More than five decades of Rosehill College, from 1970 to today. Life at Rosehill College Academic excellence, arts, sport, wellbeing and community.

title: Life At Rosehill College
description: Discover what life is like at Rosehill College! From vibrant extracurricular activities to a supportive learning environment, explore how our students thrive academically, socially, and personally. Join a community focused on growth, creativity, and achievement.
text: Our school Life at Rosehill College Rosehill College offers a vibrant and supportive environment, designed to nurture the growth and development of our students. As one of the leading secondary schools in Auckland, we provide diverse opportunities for all students to excel academically, athletically, and culturally, guiding them to achieve their personal best. Whanaungatanga RHC community and culture At Rosehill College, our House system plays a vital role in fostering a sense of community and school spirit. Each House provides students with the opportunity to contribute, grow as leaders, and celebrate their successes within a supportive and collaborative environment. This system not only encourages healthy competition but also strengthens connections among students, creating lifelong bonds. Our houses Atawhai house emblem Atawhai Kahurangi house emblem Kahurangi Manutaki house emblem Manutaki Pounamu house emblem Pounamu Rangatahi house emblem Rangatahi Taikura house emblem Taikura Academic excellence Academic excellence Rosehill College is renowned for its commitment to academic success. Our dedicated teachers inspire and challenge students to reach their full potential. We offer a comprehensive academic mentoring programme, which includes peer support, targeted tutoring, and leadership development, ensuring that every student is equipped to thrive both in and out of the classroom. Ngā toi Arts and creativity The arts are an integral part of life at Rosehill College, providing students with numerous avenues for self-expression and personal growth. From drama and dance to music and visual arts, our co-curricular programmes are designed to foster creativity and celebrate cultural diversity. Students have the chance to participate in various artistic activities, including school productions, concerts, and cultural festivals, all of which contribute to their holistic development. Ngā hākinakina Sports and recreation Sport plays a significant role in the culture of Rosehill College, promoting physical fitness, teamwork, and school spirit. We offer a wide range of sporting activities for students of all abilities, from competitive sports teams to recreational clubs. Our extensive facilities, including modern gymnasiums and multipurpose courts, provide students with the resources they need to pursue their athletic passions and develop lifelong skills. Hauora Wellbeing and support Student wellbeing is at the heart of everything we do at Rosehill College. We strive to create an inclusive and nurturing environment where all students feel safe, respected, and valued. Our pastoral care system includes dedicated guidance counsellors, peer support programmes, and a focus on student mental health, ensuring that every student has access to the support they need to succeed. Giving back Community engagement Rosehill College believes in the importance of giving back and making a positive impact on the community. Our students are encouraged to engage in various service activities, including volunteering, charity events, and environmental initiatives. These opportunities not only help students develop a sense of social responsibility but also allow them to apply their skills in meaningful ways that benefit others. Rosehill College is more than just a place of learning — it's a community where students are encouraged to grow, explore their interests, and prepare for the future with confidence. title: Principal's Message
description: Read our Principal's vision for Rosehill College. Learn about our school's values, achievements, and vision for excellence in Auckland education.
text: Te Tumuaki Principal's Message Kia ora, Talofa lava, Fakaalofa lahi atu, Nisa bula vinaka, Kia Orana, Ni Sa, Namaste, Mālō e lelei, Welcome, Herzlich Willkommen, Witamy — greetings and welcome to you all! Grounded in this whenua Rosehill College is a co-educational secondary school that sits within the Ngāti Tamaoho rohe. This area extends from north of the Tamaki Isthmus to south of the Waikato River, to the Whangamarino wetlands. It extends from the West Coast to the Hauraki Gulf/Firth of Thames. Ngāti Tamaoho has a rich cultural heritage, including traditions, stories, and practices passed down through generations. They have a significant connection to their land and natural resources, and like other iwi, they play an active role in the social, cultural, and economic life of their communities. Traditional values, innovative teaching Rosehill College is based on traditional values of Respect, Responsibility and Caring with innovative approaches to teaching and learning that is supported by modern information communication technology. It is our intention to produce graduates that are confident, self-reliant, and have self-respect and respect for others. We celebrate our coeducational and bi-cultural identity and the opportunities presented to establish healthy friendships with people from a diverse range of backgrounds which is Auckland today. For our whānau and parents of Rosehill College I welcome and encourage a strong and active parent network that will support our Values and Vision of the school. Mā tātau hangahanga ai te taiao hiranga Together we create an environment for personal excellence. Davida Suasua — Principal, Rosehill College Tīmata tō haerenga Come and see who we are Explore our values, our history and life at Rosehill College — or start your enrolment today. title: Start of School Information
description: Term Dates, Daily Times, Student Handbook, School Buses, School Map, Year 10 Camp, Rosehill College Way, Start of Year Letter to Parents, Stationery List, Uniform, BYOD
text: Te tīmatanga o te tau Start of school information We look forward to welcoming all students back to school in February 2026, and a special welcome to all our new students. Everything whānau need for the first days of the year is here — start dates, daily times, downloads and stationery lists. Start dates 2026 All Year 9 students and those new to Rosehill College will start on Monday 2 February. All other students will begin on Tuesday 3 February. Each group of students is expected to attend school for the whole time on their allocated day. Start of year dates and times 2026 Who Date Times Year 13 re-enrolment Tuesday 27 January 9:00am – 1:00pm New International Students' Orientation Tuesday 27 – Thursday 29 January 9:00am – 2:00pm each day Student Leaders' Camp (at Waharau) Wednesday 28 – Friday 30 January Departs 8:15am Wednesday, returns 5:00pm Friday Year 12 re-enrolment Thursday 29 January 9:00am – 1:00pm Peer Support Leaders Monday 2 February 8:00am – 3:10pm Year 9 and new students only Monday 2 February 8:30am – 3:10pm Buses will be operating. New students who are not Year 9s may leave at 12:50pm. All students on-site together Tuesday 3 February 9:45am – 3:10pm Getting ready for day one Daily times Classes begin at 8:50am, so please ensure your child arrives with time to be in their classroom by the beginning of the school day. Tuesday starts later, with no Period 1. School buses Buses commence their normal runs from the morning of Monday 2 February and pick students up from college at 3:20pm. Manaakitia Mai group Your student's 2026 Manaakitia Mai group is on the emailed version of the start-of-year letter — students need to know this for their first day at school. Picking up students Please arrange to meet your child off site after school. Buses and hundreds of students on foot use the front entrance, so cars cannot come in at pick-up time.
